difficulty_factors: Curcumin (turmeric dye) is one of the most persistent natural dyes known — extremely difficult to remove
Curcumin is oil-soluble — dissolves in fat, not water. Oil must be cleared first.
Multiple components: cooking oil, turmeric curcumin, spice proteins, starch from flour/thickener
Standard bleach alone is ineffective on curcumin — UV light is the critical accelerator
Alkali turns curcumin RED (deeper stain) — keep chemistry neutral to acidic

diy.title: Curry on White Cotton
ingredients: Enzyme detergent (Tide or Zout — for protein and starch)
Direct sunlight (CRITICAL — the most important tool for curcumin)
mixSteps: Scrape off surface curry first — do not rub
Do NOT use ammonia or alkaline cleaner — alkali turns curcumin bright red/deeper
Have dish soap ready for oil step, peroxide for color step
Plan for sunlight exposure — this is NOT optional for curcumin

steps.instruction: Scrape off all surface curry with a spoon. Do NOT rub — turmeric pigment spreads instantly. Flush from the back with COLD water.
steps.why: Solid removal minimizes the stain footprint. Rubbing drives curcumin (the yellow-orange dye in turmeric) deeper into cotton fibers. Cold water flushes water-soluble components without setting anything.

steps.instruction: Apply a drop of Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work in very gently. Let sit 5 minutes. Rinse with cool water.
steps.why: Curry is cooked in oil or ghee (clarified butter). Curcumin is OIL-SOLUBLE — it dissolves in fat, not water. If you skip the surfactant step, the oil traps the curcumin against the fiber and water-based chemistry can't reach it. Clearing the oil phase is essential before anything else.

steps.instruction: Apply enzyme detergent (Zout or Tide + cool water). Let sit 15–30 minutes. Rinse.
steps.why: Curry contains proteins from meat, legumes, or dairy (paneer), plus starch from flour or thickeners. Protease and amylase enzymes digest these components. Clearing the protein and starch matrix exposes the curcumin dye directly to the oxidizer in the next step.

steps.instruction: Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% generously to the remaining yellow/orange stain. While the fabric is still damp with peroxide, place it in DIRECT SUNLIGHT for 30–60 minutes. Check progress every 15 minutes.
steps.why: THIS IS THE CRITICAL STEP. Curcumin is PHOTODEGRADABLE — UV light from sunlight breaks curcumin's conjugated diketone structure. The sequence rules specifically state: 'Curcumin is photodegradable — UV light breaks curcumin's conjugated chain system. Apply H₂O₂, expose to direct sunlight. The UV+oxidizer combination is dramatically more effective than either alone.' Peroxide in the dark = slow. Sunlight alone = slow. Peroxide + sunlight = dramatically effective.

steps.instruction: After sun exposure, rinse and inspect. If yellow remains, apply fresh peroxide and return to sunlight. Repeat 2–3 times if needed.
steps.why: Curcumin stains often need multiple rounds of peroxide + UV. Each round degrades more of the chromophore. Don't escalate to harsher chemistry — just repeat the effective approach.

steps.instruction: Wash in warm water with enzyme detergent. Air dry. Inspect before using dryer.
steps.why: Heat from dryer can set any residual curcumin permanently. Verify removal first.

diy.ifStillThere: OxiClean soak (warm water, 1–6 hours) followed by sunlight exposure. For white cotton specifically: dilute chlorine bleach (1:10) COMBINED with sunlight exposure is the nuclear option. Curcumin is one of the most stubborn natural dyes — patience and UV light are the path, not stronger chemistry.
proTips: SUNLIGHT is the single most effective tool for curcumin. No product matches it.
Do NOT use ammonia or alkaline cleaners on curry stains — alkaline pH turns curcumin BRIGHT RED, deepening the stain visibly.
Curcumin is oil-soluble — the oil must be cleared FIRST or nothing else works.
Multiple rounds of peroxide + sun beat one application of strong bleach.

Dan Eisen treats mustard (which contains turmeric) by scraping first, then following the tannin procedure — but the curcumin component often requires bleaching beyond what standard tannin treatment achieves.
diy.safety: Peroxide 3% is safe on white cotton. Sunlight exposure of cotton is safe short-term. NEVER mix ammonia with chlorine bleach. Test any colored cotton for dye safety before peroxide.
diy.whyThisWorks: Curry stains derive their persistent color from curcumin (turmeric), a natural dye with a conjugated diketone chromophore. This chromophore is photosensitive — UV radiation provides the activation energy to break the conjugated bonds, destroying the yellow color. Hydrogen peroxide provides additional oxidative power. The combination of H₂O₂ + UV is dramatically more effective than either alone because UV generates reactive oxygen species from peroxide that attack curcumin from multiple angles.

home.title: Curry on White Cotton — Challenging but Achievable Removal
home.confidenceNote: Curcumin is one of the most persistent natural dyes. Fresh stains with immediate treatment have good odds. Aged or heat-set curry stains may leave a faint shadow even after professional treatment. UV sunlight exposure is the critical success factor.

primaryProduct.name: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover
primaryProduct.why: Sustained peroxide generation — apply paste, expose to sunlight for photocatalytic curcumin destruction

home.prepStep: Scrape off curry. Do NOT rub. Flush from back with cold water. Clear oil FIRST.

method.instruction: Scrape off curry. Flush from back with cold water. Apply Dawn to oil residue, work 5 min, rinse.
method.why: Curcumin is oil-soluble — clearing the oil carrier is the essential first step.

method.instruction: Apply Zout Triple Enzyme, let sit 15–30 min, rinse.
method.why: Enzymes clear protein and starch matrix, exposing the curcumin dye for oxidation.

method.instruction: Apply OxiClean paste to the yellow stain. Place fabric in direct sunlight for 30–60 minutes.
method.why: UV + oxidizer = the only effective combination for curcumin. This is the critical step.

method.instruction: Rinse, reapply OxiClean paste if yellow remains, return to sunlight. Repeat 2–3 times.
method.why: Curcumin typically needs 2–3 rounds. Patience + UV is the path.

method.instruction: Wash warm with enzyme detergent. Air dry. Inspect before dryer.
method.why: Heat sets residual curcumin. Verify removal before applying heat.

materialWarnings.warning: Full chemistry is safe — but ammonia/alkaline cleaners turn curcumin BRIGHT RED. Avoid alkali on this stain.
Extended sunlight exposure is safe for cotton. Do not exceed 2 hours to prevent fiber degradation.
DANGER: Never mix ammonia with chlorine bleach. Produces toxic chloramine gas.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: If stain survives 3 rounds of peroxide + sunlight on white cotton: professional sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach) treatment. Curcumin may also respond to professional-grade oxidation at controlled temperature.

proTips: Sunlight, sunlight, sunlight. It's the single most important tool for curcumin.
Keep the stain damp with peroxide during UV exposure — dry fabric doesn't photodegrade as effectively.
If it's cloudy: a UV nail-curing lamp or UV-C light can substitute for sunlight in a pinch.
home.whyThisWorks: Curcumin's chromophore is a conjugated diketone system. UV radiation provides the activation energy to break these conjugated bonds. H₂O₂ provides reactive oxygen species. Together, they decompose curcumin into smaller, colorless fragments.
home.timeEstimate: 30–90 min active treatment + UV exposure time

pro.title: Curry on White Cotton — Professional Combination Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Combination (oil + dye + protein + starch). The dominant challenge is CURCUMIN (diferuloylmethane, C₂₁H₂₀O₆) — the primary pigment in turmeric. Curcumin is a polyphenol with a conjugated diketone chromophore that absorbs at ~425nm (bright yellow). It is: (1) Oil-soluble (dissolves in fats, not water), (2) Photodegradable (UV cleaves the conjugated system), (3) pH-sensitive (turns RED at pH >8.5 due to deprotonation — alkali makes the stain WORSE), (4) Extremely substantive to cellulose. Other components: cooking oil/ghee (triglyceride), spice proteins, starch from flour/thickener. Dan Eisen mustard article (#58): turmeric gives mustard its color. 'Using soap, ammonia and alkali. Even color safe bleaches contain alkali which can set the stain making it difficult or even impossible to remove. When you use an alkali on a mustard stain you can immediately see the staining turning a deep purple color.' White cotton: full chemistry safe, pH 6.0–11.0, BUT avoid high pH specifically for curcumin.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape off all surface curry. Do NOT brush vigorously — curcumin spreads and stains instantly. Gentle, one-direction scraping.
Cold flush from back. Removes water-soluble components (dissolved spices, salt, acid). Curcumin itself will NOT flush with water — it's oil-soluble.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dissolve oil carrier — curcumin is trapped in fat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG or concentrated surfactant. Curcumin dissolves in oil/ghee — the cooking fat is the carrier that holds curcumin against the fiber. Clearing this oil phase is PREREQUISITE to all subsequent treatment. Without this step, water-based chemistry cannot reach the curcumin. Tamp with brush. Flush with VDS or water.

spottingProtocol.technique: Enzyme digestion of protein and starch components
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply enzyme digester for protein (meat, dairy, legume) and starch (flour, thickener) components. Clear the biological matrix so curcumin dye is directly exposed. Safe on cotton. 15–30 min contact time at 30–40°C.

spottingProtocol.technique: Break tannin bonds from spice polyphenols other than curcumin
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply tannin formula for the non-curcumin polyphenol components of the curry spice blend. Curry contains coriander, cumin, fenugreek — all have tannin/polyphenol content. The acidic pH is beneficial: curcumin is more stable at acidic pH (yellow) vs alkaline pH (red). Flush with water. Neutralize acid on cellulose.

spottingProtocol.technique: Photocatalytic oxidation — THE CRITICAL STEP for curcumin
spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–10 min peroxide contact, then 30–60 min direct sunlight
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply H₂O₂ 6% to the remaining yellow stain. Do NOT add ammonia — the alkaline pH would turn curcumin red. Apply peroxide at neutral to slightly acidic pH. Then expose the damp, peroxide-treated fabric to DIRECT SUNLIGHT for 30–60 minutes. Per sequence rules: 'Curcumin is photodegradable — UV light breaks curcumin's conjugated chain system. Apply H₂O₂, expose to direct sunlight. The UV+oxidizer combination is dramatically more effective than either alone.' This is not optional — peroxide in the dark is far less effective on curcumin. UV photons provide the activation energy to generate reactive oxygen species from H₂O₂ that attack curcumin's conjugated diketone system from multiple angles.

spottingProtocol.agent: Cold water flush + repeat if needed
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush. Inspect. Curcumin typically requires 2–3 rounds of peroxide + UV. Apply fresh peroxide each round — the H₂O₂ decomposes during UV exposure. On white cotton, each round progressively lightens the stain. Continue until yellow is gone or no further improvement is seen.

pro.escalation: If curcumin persists after 3 rounds of H₂O₂ + UV: (1) Sodium hypochlorite (chlorine bleach) 1:10 on white cotton, 15 min — chlorine is effective on curcumin but harsher. (2) Sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach) — attacks the chromophore by reduction rather than oxidation. Dan Eisen's reducing bleach article (#89): 'Reducing bleaches are used primarily for dye and last traces of stains containing dye.' (3) Potassium permanganate followed by sodium bisulfite — professional-level oxidation/reduction cycle.
pro.customerExplanation: Curry gets its distinctive yellow color from turmeric, which contains curcumin — one of the most persistent natural dyes in the world. Standard stain removers can't touch it because curcumin doesn't dissolve in water and doesn't respond to normal bleach alone. The key is UV light from sunlight combined with hydrogen peroxide — curcumin is naturally sensitive to UV radiation, and the peroxide amplifies the effect. We treated the oil and food components first, then used controlled UV + peroxide cycles to break down the yellow pigment.
commonMistakes: Using ammonia or alkaline cleaner — immediately turns curcumin BRIGHT RED, visibly deepening the stain
Using bleach without UV exposure — peroxide alone is far less effective on curcumin than peroxide + sunlight
Skipping the oil removal step — curcumin is oil-soluble and trapped in the cooking fat against the fiber
Rubbing curry into the fabric — curcumin spreads and stains instantly on contact
Expecting one treatment to work — curcumin almost always needs 2–3 oxidation + UV cycles
pro.neutralization: Neutral to slightly acidic final rinse — avoid alkaline residue that would shift residual curcumin toward red. Thorough flush between chemistry changes.
pro.fabricRisks: White cotton: full chemistry safe EXCEPT avoid high pH specifically for this stain (curcumin turns red at pH >8.5). UV exposure safe short-term (under 2 hours). Chlorine bleach safe as last resort on whites.
